scene_description: This slide illustrates the client - server communication model, where **users** initiate a *Request* to the **Server**, and the server sends back a *Response* to fulfill the request. The key points are the bidirectional flow of requests from users to the server and responses from the server to users.

scene_description: This slide explains **horizontal scaling**, a method where multiple server replicas (labeled "Replicas") handle user requests, with bidirectional arrows illustrating communication between the replicas and users to distribute workload across added instances.
description: This slide explains **horizontal scaling**, a method where multiple server replicas (labeled "Replicas") handle user requests, with bidirectional arrows illustrating communication between the replicas and users to distribute workload across added instances.

scene_description: This slide illustrates **replica server status and user interaction** in a distributed system, showing a failed replica (red “X”), a healthy replica (green checkmark), and active replicas communicating bidirectionally with users via arrows. Key points include visualizing replica health states and the flow of requests/responses between replicas and end users.
description: This slide illustrates **replica server status and user interaction** in a distributed system, showing a failed replica (red “X”), a healthy replica (green checkmark), and active replicas communicating bidirectionally with users via arrows. Key points include the worldwide server distribution and interconnected routes enabling optimized content delivery.

scene_description: The slide illustrates **Leader-Leader Replication**, where two leader nodes each handle both read and write operations while replicating data between themselves, enabling bidirectional synchronization. Key points include dual leadership (no single point of failure for writes) and mutual replication between the two leader databases.

scene_description: This slide explains the CAP theorem in distributed systems, where "C" (Consistency), "A" (Availability), and "P" (Partition Tolerance) form a triangle, emphasizing the principle that a system can only guarantee two of these three properties at once ("Pick two of three").
description: This slide explains the CAP theorem in distributed systems, where "C" (Consistency), "A" (Availability), and "P" (Partition Tolerance) form a triangle, emphasizing the principle that a system can only guarantee two of these three properties at once ("Pick two of three").

scene_description: This slide introduces the concepts of replication and sharding, focusing on a database replication setup where a **Leader** node handles read/write operations from one client and replicates data to a **Follower** node, which serves read operations for another client. Key points include the role of the Leader in managing primary data and the Follower in mirroring that data via replication.
